APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—improving word representations by incorporating n-grams—is squarely within NLP/ML and targets the community that develops embeddings. The paper uses standard methods and baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), common evaluation datasets (Google/MSR/Levy et al.) and established similarity/analogy metrics, so the methodology and evaluation meet venue expectations. Limitations (inconsistent gains on similarity tasks, increased computational costs for ngram-ngram stats, and lack of hyperparameter tuning for ngrams) are acknowledged and suggest minor scope/robustness concerns but do not undermine venue relevance. Code availability and adequate related-work coverage further support suitability for a top conference. | B(5): The paper addresses core NLP/representation-learning topics (word embeddings with ngram contexts) that are squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and of interest to the venue audience. It formulates the problem in standard terms, extends well-known methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and proposes a practical, efficiency-oriented matrix-construction strategy that targets realistic hardware constraints. Evaluation uses established datasets and metrics (Google/MSR analogies, Levy et al. similarity/analogy sets) and code/hyperparameters are provided, indicating the methodological and empirical standards expected at a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—improving word representations by incorporating n-grams—is squarely within NLP/ML and targets the community that develops embeddings. The paper uses standard methods and baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), common evaluation datasets (Google/MSR/Levy et al.) and established similarity/analogy metrics, so the methodology and evaluation meet venue expectations. Limitations (inconsistent gains on similarity tasks, increased computational costs for ngram-ngram stats, and lack of hyperparameter tuning for ngrams) are acknowledged and suggest minor scope/robustness concerns but do not undermine venue relevance. Code availability and adequate related-work coverage further support suitability for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses core NLP/representation-learning topics (word embeddings with ngram contexts) that are squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and of interest to the venue audience. It formulates the problem in standard terms, extends well-known methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and proposes a practical, efficiency-oriented matrix-construction strategy that targets realistic hardware constraints. Evaluation uses established datasets and metrics (Google/MSR analogies, Levy et al. similarity/analogy sets) and code/hyperparameters are provided, indicating the methodological and empirical standards expected at a top conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its approach across multiple embedding algorithms (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and datasets (Google and MSR analogy sets plus the Levy et al. similarity/analogy collections), measuring both similarity and analogy performance and showing the claimed improvements (particularly on analogy tasks). The authors include ablation-style findings (e.g., strong gains from bi-grams, negligible benefit but high cost from ngram–ngram co-occurrences) and report failure cases (SVD sometimes worsens, similarity gains inconsistent). Reproducibility is supported (code and hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is adequate, but they explicitly did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams and leave sensitivity analyses of hyperparameters and ngram choices for future work, which is a notable gap. | B(4): The paper runs experiments across multiple standard benchmarks (Google/MSR analogy and Levy et al. similarity/analogy sets) and compares ngram-augmented versions of four strong baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), using both similarity and analogy metrics, which supports the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partial: the authors report negative results (e.g., ngram–ngram cost, bi-grams helping analogies but not similarity, SVD degradation) and note hyperparameters were not tuned for ngrams, indicating gaps in deeper sensitivity studies. Related work coverage is described as adequate, failure cases and limitations are discussed, and reproducibility is well supported (code available and hyperparameters reported). Overall this is solid experimental work with minor gaps in hyperparameter/sensitivity exploration.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its approach across multiple embedding algorithms (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and datasets (Google and MSR analogy sets plus the Levy et al. similarity/analogy collections), measuring both similarity and analogy performance and showing the claimed improvements (particularly on analogy tasks). The authors include ablation-style findings (e.g., strong gains from bi-grams, negligible benefit but high cost from ngram–ngram co-occurrences) and report failure cases (SVD sometimes worsens, similarity gains inconsistent). Reproducibility is supported (code and hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is adequate, but they explicitly did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams and leave sensitivity analyses of hyperparameters and ngram choices for future work, which is a notable gap.

Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partial: the authors report negative results (e.g., ngram–ngram cost, bi-grams helping analogies but not similarity, SVD degradation) and note hyperparameters were not tuned for ngrams, indicating gaps in deeper sensitivity studies. Related work coverage is described as adequate, failure cases and limitations are discussed, and reproducibility is well supported (code available and hyperparameters reported). Overall this is solid experimental work with minor gaps in hyperparameter/sensitivity exploration.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable extension by systematically introducing n‑grams as context units across four foundational embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD) rather than a single model, which is a non-trivial generalization. It also proposes a novel, more efficient n‑gram co‑occurrence matrix construction strategy (mixing ‘mixture’ and ‘stripes’) to reduce hardware costs, a practical contribution that differentiates it from prior work. Given the scope of methods addressed and the engineering efficiency gains, the contribution is clearly differentiated and informative to experts, though it is more an important extension than a radically new paradigm. | B(3): The paper applies ngram contexts systematically across both predictive (SGNS) and count-based (GloVe, PPMI, SVD) word-representation methods and proposes a novel, more efficient ngram co-occurrence matrix construction strategy — which is a useful engineering contribution. However, the underlying problem (improving word embeddings with subword/ngram information) is well-studied, and the core idea is an incremental extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors clearly differentiate their multi-method application and efficiency technique, but the novelty is moderate rather than highly surprising to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable extension by systematically introducing n‑grams as context units across four foundational embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD) rather than a single model, which is a non-trivial generalization. It also proposes a novel, more efficient n‑gram co‑occurrence matrix construction strategy (mixing ‘mixture’ and ‘stripes’) to reduce hardware costs, a practical contribution that differentiates it from prior work. Given the scope of methods addressed and the engineering efficiency gains, the contribution is clearly differentiated and informative to experts, though it is more an important extension than a radically new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies ngram contexts systematically across both predictive (SGNS) and count-based (GloVe, PPMI, SVD) word-representation methods and proposes a novel, more efficient ngram co-occurrence matrix construction strategy — which is a useful engineering contribution. However, the underlying problem (improving word embeddings with subword/ngram information) is well-studied, and the core idea is an incremental extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors clearly differentiate their multi-method application and efficiency technique, but the novelty is moderate rather than highly surprising to experts.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a practical problem—improving word representations—by integrating ngrams into four widely-used methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and demonstrates clear gains on analogy tasks while producing ngram embeddings that capture semantic and syntactic patterns. Its proposed efficient co-occurrence matrix construction (mixing the ‘mixture’ and ‘stripes’ strategies) and released code lower hardware barriers, increasing the likelihood practitioners will try and extend the approach. However, improvements are uneven (little benefit from ngram–ngram statistics, inconsistent effects on similarity tasks, and occasional degradation for SVD) and hyperparameters were not fully explored, which limits broad or transformative impact. | B(3): The paper tackles a relevant problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngrams across major methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD)—and demonstrates measurable gains, especially on analogy benchmarks (Google/MSR and datasets from Levy et al.). Its proposed efficient matrix-construction strategy that reduces memory/disk requirements and enables running ngram-based models on modest hardware increases practical usefulness and likelihood of follow-up work. However, mixed results (limited or no improvement on similarity tasks, occasional degradation for SVD) and the high cost of ngram–ngram statistics constrain its immediate, field‑changing impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a practical problem—improving word representations—by integrating ngrams into four widely-used methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and demonstrates clear gains on analogy tasks while producing ngram embeddings that capture semantic and syntactic patterns. Its proposed efficient co-occurrence matrix construction (mixing the ‘mixture’ and ‘stripes’ strategies) and released code lower hardware barriers, increasing the likelihood practitioners will try and extend the approach. However, improvements are uneven (little benefit from ngram–ngram statistics, inconsistent effects on similarity tasks, and occasional degradation for SVD) and hyperparameters were not fully explored, which limits broad or transformative impact.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a relevant problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngrams across major methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD)—and demonstrates measurable gains, especially on analogy benchmarks (Google/MSR and datasets from Levy et al.). Its proposed efficient matrix-construction strategy that reduces memory/disk requirements and enables running ngram-based models on modest hardware increases practical usefulness and likelihood of follow-up work. However, mixed results (limited or no improvement on similarity tasks, occasional degradation for SVD) and the high cost of ngram–ngram statistics constrain its immediate, field‑changing impact.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and evaluates on common datasets (Google and MSR analogy sets and the Levy et al. similarity/analogy datasets) with appropriate metrics. The authors report hyperparameters and code, acknowledge when ngram variants bring little improvement or hurt performance (e.g., SVD with bi-grams), and discuss computational trade-offs, indicating awareness of prior methods performing comparably or better. A minor fairness concern remains because they state they did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams, which could affect the strength of some comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and standard baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) using canonical evaluation sets (Google and MSR analogies and Levy et al. datasets) and reports hyperparameters and code, indicating largely fair comparisons. However, the authors acknowledge they did not tune ngram-specific hyperparameters (which could affect results) and report cases (e.g., SVD with bigrams) where prior methods perform comparably or better; they also analyze computational cost trade-offs, so overall comparisons are strong but with minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and evaluates on common datasets (Google and MSR analogy sets and the Levy et al. similarity/analogy datasets) with appropriate metrics. The authors report hyperparameters and code, acknowledge when ngram variants bring little improvement or hurt performance (e.g., SVD with bi-grams), and discuss computational trade-offs, indicating awareness of prior methods performing comparably or better. A minor fairness concern remains because they state they did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams, which could affect the strength of some comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and standard baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) using canonical evaluation sets (Google and MSR analogies and Levy et al. datasets) and reports hyperparameters and code, indicating largely fair comparisons. However, the authors acknowledge they did not tune ngram-specific hyperparameters (which could affect results) and report cases (e.g., SVD with bigrams) where prior methods perform comparably or better; they also analyze computational cost trade-offs, so overall comparisons are strong but with minor omissions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are generally supported: authors compare ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD against appropriate baselines on standard analogy and similarity datasets and report consistent improvements on analogy tasks. Mathematical framework and the proposed efficient matrix-construction strategy are described and plausible, and code/hyperparameters are provided. Minor concerns remain: the authors did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams (which they acknowledge) and no mention of statistical significance testing is reported, and some methods (SVD) degrade with bi-grams, indicating results are sensitive to settings. Overall these are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental conclusions. | B(3): The paper provides empirical evidence on standard analogy and similarity datasets and compares against appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), and shows clear gains on analogy tasks; however improvements are inconsistent (little or negative effect on similarity tasks and SVD) and the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams. Mathematical extensions appear plausible from the summary, but the experimental methodology has gaps (no mention of statistical tests, limited hyperparameter search) and the claimed efficiency of the novel matrix-construction strategy is not quantified in the provided summary. Given these issues, the technical claims are partially supported but important evidence and controls are missing, so a moderate score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are generally supported: authors compare ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD against appropriate baselines on standard analogy and similarity datasets and report consistent improvements on analogy tasks. Mathematical framework and the proposed efficient matrix-construction strategy are described and plausible, and code/hyperparameters are provided. Minor concerns remain: the authors did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams (which they acknowledge) and no mention of statistical significance testing is reported, and some methods (SVD) degrade with bi-grams, indicating results are sensitive to settings. Overall these are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides empirical evidence on standard analogy and similarity datasets and compares against appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), and shows clear gains on analogy tasks; however improvements are inconsistent (little or negative effect on similarity tasks and SVD) and the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams. Mathematical extensions appear plausible from the summary, but the experimental methodology has gaps (no mention of statistical tests, limited hyperparameter search) and the claimed efficiency of the novel matrix-construction strategy is not quantified in the provided summary. Given these issues, the technical claims are partially supported but important evidence and controls are missing, so a moderate score is warranted.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized (intro, method, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions early — introducing ngrams into SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD and a more efficient co-occurrence matrix construction — and evaluates them on standard datasets (Google/MSR/Levy et al.). Notation and methodological choices appear to be described with enough detail to report hyperparameters and enable code release, and related work coverage is adequate. Presentation has minor issues: writing quality is described as only "adequate," and the authors note unresolved hyperparameter relationships and mixed results (e.g., SVD with bi-grams), which suggest some explanations or examples could be clearer. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and presents its approach (adding ngrams into SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD and an efficient matrix-construction strategy) with a clear method→experiments→conclusion flow. Main contributions are stated early and backed by experiments on analogy/similarity datasets, with hyperparameters reported and code available, which aids reproducibility. Writing, notation, and related-work coverage are described as adequate, so only minor presentation issues remain (e.g., some mixed results like SVD with bigrams and unexplored hyperparameter relationships could be explained more clearly).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (intro, method, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions early — introducing ngrams into SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD and a more efficient co-occurrence matrix construction — and evaluates them on standard datasets (Google/MSR/Levy et al.). Notation and methodological choices appear to be described with enough detail to report hyperparameters and enable code release, and related work coverage is adequate. Presentation has minor issues: writing quality is described as only "adequate," and the authors note unresolved hyperparameter relationships and mixed results (e.g., SVD with bi-grams), which suggest some explanations or examples could be clearer.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and presents its approach (adding ngrams into SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD and an efficient matrix-construction strategy) with a clear method→experiments→conclusion flow. Main contributions are stated early and backed by experiments on analogy/similarity datasets, with hyperparameters reported and code available, which aids reproducibility. Writing, notation, and related-work coverage are described as adequate, so only minor presentation issues remain (e.g., some mixed results like SVD with bigrams and unexplored hyperparameter relationships could be explained more clearly).

main_claims: Introduce ngrams into four word representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI matrix, and SVD) to improve word representations.
Experimental results demonstrate that using ngram co-occurrence statistics yields improved word representations, especially on analogy tasks.
Trained ngram embeddings reflect semantic meanings and syntactic patterns (useful for tasks like finding antonyms and collocations).
Propose a novel, more efficient way of building ngram co-occurrence matrices to reduce hardware costs and enable running ngram-based models on modest hardware.
_summary.methodology: Treat ngrams as additional context units by adding ngrams into the context vocabulary for SGNS and extend analogous ngram-based co-occurrence statistics to GloVe, PPMI and SVD; additionally propose an efficient matrix-construction strategy (mixing ‘mixture’ and ‘stripes’ strategies) to reduce memory/disk requirements.
datasets: Datasets from Levy et al. (2015) (six similarity and two analogy datasets)
metrics: Analogy tasks (additive and multiplicative evaluation; accuracies)
limitations: Using ngram-ngram co-occurrence statistics brings little improvement but immense computational costs.
Improvements from bi-grams are strong for analogy tasks but not consistently observed on similarity tasks.
In some cases (SVD), adding bi-grams led to worse results; authors note they did not tune hyperparameters for ngrams and suspect default settings may be to blame.
Authors state relationships between ngrams and various hyperparameters require further exploration (future work).
